stage-m13.text: CVE-2016-1287 was a heap overflow in Cisco ASA's handling of which protocol?
options: IKE (Internet Key Exchange) for VPN tunnel setup
stage-m13.explanation: The flaw lived in the ASA's fragmented IKE packet reassembly used to negotiate VPN tunnels.

stage-m13.text: What authentication did an attacker need to trigger the CVE-2016-1287 heap overflow?
stage-m13.explanation: The overflow occurred during IKE negotiation, before any authentication — making it pre-auth.

stage-m13.text: Which ports exposed the CVE-2016-1287 attack surface on Cisco ASA?
stage-m13.explanation: IKE listens on UDP/500 and UDP/4500 (NAT traversal), both reachable on internet-facing VPN firewalls.

stage-m13.text: The Shadow Brokers' EXTRABACON tool exploited this flaw. Who is believed to have originally developed it?
stage-m13.explanation: EXTRABACON came from the NSA toolset leaked by the Shadow Brokers in 2016.

stage-m13.text: On 64-bit Cisco ASA platforms, what was the typical result of CVE-2016-1287?
options: Denial of service (device reload), not reliable RCE
stage-m13.explanation: On 64-bit platforms the overflow usually crashed/reloaded the device rather than yielding clean RCE.

stage-m13.text: Why is a VPN-terminating firewall like the ASA such a high-value target for a flaw like this?
options: It sits at the internet edge and a crash or compromise disrupts or exposes all remote access
stage-m13.explanation: Perimeter VPN devices are internet-reachable and central to remote access, magnifying impact.

stage-m13.text: What was the correct remediation for CVE-2016-1287?
options: Apply Cisco's patched ASA software; restrict IKE exposure where possible
stage-m13.explanation: Patching the IKE reassembly code was the fix; limiting UDP/500/4500 exposure reduced surface meanwhile.

stage-m13.text: Why are pre-authentication vulnerabilities like CVE-2016-1287 especially dangerous?
options: No credentials are needed, so any internet host can attempt exploitation
stage-m13.explanation: Pre-auth flaws are reachable by anyone on the internet, removing the credential barrier entirely.

stage-m14.text: CVE-2017-3881 exploited the Telnet option parser of which Cisco protocol?
stage-m14.explanation: The flaw was in IOS's handling of a CMP-specific Telnet option, leading to a stack overflow.

stage-m14.text: Was CVE-2017-3881 only exploitable if Telnet was explicitly enabled?
options: No — the vulnerable CMP parsing ran even when Telnet was disabled in config
Yes, Telnet had to be on
stage-m14.explanation: The CMP option code processed regardless of the Telnet config, so disabling Telnet didn't help.

stage-m14.text: How was CVE-2017-3881 brought to public attention?
options: WikiLeaks published Vault 7, which referenced a Cisco CMP exploit
stage-m14.explanation: The Vault 7 CIA leak referenced the CMP exploit, prompting Cisco's investigation and advisory.

stage-m14.text: Roughly how long had this vulnerability existed in Cisco IOS before it was patched?
stage-m14.explanation: The vulnerable code dated to roughly 1996, making it a 20+ year latent flaw.

stage-m14.text: What was the correct mitigation for CVE-2017-3881 before patching?
options: A vty ACL blocking TCP/23, because the vulnerable code ran regardless of 'no service telnet'
stage-m14.explanation: Since the code ran even with Telnet 'disabled,' a vty ACL blocking TCP/23 was required.

stage-m14.text: Why did 'no service telnet' fail to protect against CVE-2017-3881?
options: The vulnerable CMP option parser executed independently of the Telnet service state
stage-m14.explanation: The parser ran before/independent of the service toggle, so the config provided no real protection.

stage-m14.text: What did successful exploitation of CVE-2017-3881 give an attacker?
options: Code execution / control of the IOS switch before authentication
stage-m14.explanation: The stack overflow enabled pre-auth code execution, owning the device.

stage-m14.text: What lesson does a 20-year-old latent flaw like this teach?
options: Legacy code paths can carry exploitable bugs for decades until scrutinized
New code is always the risk
stage-m14.explanation: Long-lived, rarely-reviewed code can hide critical vulnerabilities for a very long time.

stage-m15.text: CDPwn / CVE-2020-3118 was a format string vulnerability triggered by what in a CDP packet?
options: A malicious format specifier (e.g. %n) in the Device-ID TLV field
stage-m15.explanation: A format specifier in the CDP Device-ID TLV reached a vulnerable formatting routine.

stage-m15.text: Can a properly configured firewall block CDP-based attacks like CDPwn?
options: No — CDP is a Layer 2 protocol; firewalls and ACLs don't apply
stage-m15.explanation: CDP operates at Layer 2, so Layer 3 firewalls/ACLs provide no protection — segmentation is needed.

stage-m15.text: Which Cisco OS did CVE-2020-3118 specifically target, common on carrier-grade routers?
options: Cisco IOS XR — used on ASR 9000 and NCS 5000 series
stage-m15.explanation: CVE-2020-3118 hit IOS XR on high-end carrier platforms like the ASR 9000 and NCS 5000.

stage-m15.text: Which security firm disclosed the CDPwn suite in February 2020?
stage-m15.explanation: Armis disclosed the five CDPwn zero-days across four Cisco operating systems.

stage-m15.text: Why is the %n format specifier dangerous?
options: It writes the count of bytes printed to an address, enabling arbitrary memory writes
stage-m15.explanation: %n writes a value to a pointed-to address — an attacker-controlled write primitive.

stage-m15.text: Besides patching, what limits exposure to a Layer 2 protocol attack like CDPwn?
options: Disabling CDP on untrusted ports and segmenting Layer 2 domains
stage-m15.explanation: Disabling CDP where unneeded and tight L2 segmentation reduce the attack surface.

stage-m15.text: Why was CDPwn described as turning Cisco devices into a 'pivot point'?
options: Code execution on infrastructure devices lets attackers move laterally across the network
stage-m15.explanation: Owning switches/routers via CDP gives a foothold to pivot deeper into the network.

stage-m15.text: Why did CDP being enabled by default amplify CDPwn's risk?
options: It was running on vast numbers of devices, expanding the exploitable population
stage-m15.explanation: Default-on CDP meant a huge installed base was potentially vulnerable.

stage-m16.text: CVE-2021-1609 affected the web management interface of which Cisco product line?
options: Cisco RV340/RV345 — small/medium business VPN routers
stage-m16.explanation: The flaw was in the web UI of the RV340/RV345 SMB router family.

stage-m16.text: Did CVE-2021-1609 require a valid admin password to reach the vulnerable endpoint?
options: No — the /api/v1/diag_* endpoints were processed before authentication
stage-m16.explanation: The diagnostic API endpoints handled requests prior to the auth check, enabling pre-auth exploitation.

stage-m16.text: Why was OS command injection on the RV340/RV345 so impactful?
options: It ran on Linux (BusyBox) as root with no privilege separation
It ran sandboxed as a guest user
It needed a second exploit for root
stage-m16.explanation: Code executed as root on the BusyBox Linux base, giving full device control immediately.

stage-m16.text: Which threat actors were documented using CVE-2021-1609 as initial access?
options: Ransomware groups using compromised SOHO routers as persistent entry points
stage-m16.explanation: Ransomware crews leveraged vulnerable branch-office routers for durable initial access.

stage-m16.text: What was the only effective mitigation besides the firmware patch?
options: Disabling remote management to remove the exposed attack surface
stage-m16.explanation: With no other workaround, disabling remote management eliminated external exposure until patching.

stage-m16.text: Why do SOHO routers running all services as root make injection flaws so severe?
options: Any code execution immediately gains full root control with no privilege boundary to cross
stage-m16.explanation: No privilege separation means a single injection equals total device compromise.

stage-m16.text: Why are branch-office SOHO routers attractive ransomware footholds?
options: They're internet-exposed, often unpatched, and bridge into corporate networks
stage-m16.explanation: Exposed, neglected edge devices give attackers a quiet, persistent way into the enterprise.

stage-m16.text: What is the general principle for protecting SMB router management interfaces?
options: Never expose management to the internet; restrict and patch promptly
stage-m16.explanation: Keeping management off the public internet and patching fast removes the common attack vector.

stage-m17.text: CVE-2020-3259 was a memory disclosure flaw in Cisco ASA/FTD. What did the leaked heap memory commonly contain?
options: Active VPN session tokens and cached credentials
stage-m17.explanation: The disclosure leaked sensitive heap contents including live VPN session tokens and credentials.

stage-m17.text: When did the Akira ransomware group begin exploiting CVE-2020-3259?
options: About four years after disclosure, in 2024
Within weeks of the May 2020 disclosure
stage-m17.explanation: Akira weaponized the four-year-old flaw in 2024 against unpatched VPN appliances.

stage-m17.text: How did CVE-2020-3259 specifically bypass multi-factor authentication?
options: Session token theft — a stolen active session grants access regardless of how it was obtained
stage-m17.explanation: A valid session token represents an already-authenticated session, so MFA is moot once it's stolen.

stage-m17.text: Which ASA interface did CVE-2020-3259 target?
stage-m17.explanation: The flaw was in the WebVPN/AnyConnect HTTPS services interface.

stage-m17.text: After stealing a valid VPN session token, does the attacker still need to complete MFA?
options: No — the token represents a session where MFA was already completed
stage-m17.explanation: The token was issued post-MFA, so replaying it grants authenticated access directly.

stage-m17.text: What is the core remediation for CVE-2020-3259?
options: Patch ASA/FTD and rotate/invalidate active VPN sessions and credentials
stage-m17.explanation: Patching plus invalidating potentially-stolen sessions and rotating credentials closes the exposure.

stage-m17.text: What does Akira's reuse of a 2020 CVE in 2024 illustrate?
options: Unpatched edge devices remain exploitable for years after disclosure
stage-m17.explanation: Attackers harvest long-known, unpatched vulnerabilities — patch cadence on edge gear is critical.

stage-m17.text: Why is invalidating sessions important after a memory-disclosure compromise?
options: Stolen tokens stay valid until expired/revoked, granting ongoing access
stage-m17.explanation: Unless revoked, leaked tokens keep working — so forced re-authentication is essential.

stage-m18.text: CVE-2019-1663 exploited a stack overflow in which component of the RV110W/RV130W?
options: The web-based management interface HTTP request handler
stage-m18.explanation: The overflow was in the HTTP request handler of the router's web management interface.

stage-m18.text: Did these MIPS-based routers include stack canaries and ASLR?
options: No — they lacked canaries, ASLR, and NX/DEP, making exploitation trivial
stage-m18.explanation: Absent modern exploit mitigations, a straightforward overflow yielded reliable code execution.

stage-m18.text: How large was the vulnerable buffer and what overflowed it?
options: A 256-byte buffer — 500+ bytes in the POST body triggered the overflow
stage-m18.explanation: A 256-byte stack buffer was overrun by a POST body of 500+ bytes.

stage-m18.text: What privilege level did a successful exploit achieve?
options: Root (uid=0) — SOHO routers run services as root with no separation
stage-m18.explanation: With no privilege separation, exploitation gave immediate root.

stage-m18.text: Was CVE-2019-1663 exploitable from the WAN?
options: Yes — attackers with access to the WAN-facing management interface could exploit it remotely
stage-m18.explanation: Exposed WAN-side management made remote, internet-based exploitation possible.

stage-m18.text: Why was reliable exploitation of CVE-2019-1663 so easy?
options: No canaries/ASLR/NX meant a simple overflow directly controlled execution
stage-m18.explanation: The absence of standard memory-safety mitigations removed every obstacle to exploitation.

stage-m18.text: How were vulnerable MIPS SOHO routers abused at scale in 2019?
options: Mass-exploited into botnets, notably across Asia-Pacific retail/SMB
stage-m18.explanation: Attackers herded the unmitigated routers into botnets across SMB networks.

stage-m18.text: What is the right protection for exposed SOHO router management?
options: Patch firmware and never expose the management interface to the WAN
stage-m18.explanation: Patching and removing WAN-side management exposure eliminate the remote attack path.

stage-m19.text: CVE-2022-20812 was a path traversal in which Cisco product's cluster database API?
options: Cisco Expressway Series / TelePresence VCS — video conferencing infrastructure
stage-m19.explanation: The traversal was in the cluster DB API of the Expressway / TelePresence VCS.

stage-m19.text: How do path traversal attacks operate?
options: Using '../' sequences to navigate outside the intended directory boundary
stage-m19.explanation: Directory traversal escapes the allowed root via sequences like ../../../../etc/shadow.

stage-m19.text: Beyond reading files like /etc/shadow, what did the file-write path enable?
options: Writing cron jobs or init scripts for persistent root access across reboots
stage-m19.explanation: Arbitrary write let attackers drop cron/init scripts for reboot-persistent root.

stage-m19.text: Where is Cisco Expressway typically deployed?
options: In the DMZ — handling firewall traversal and SIP/H.323 routing
Deep in the core, with no exposure
stage-m19.explanation: Expressway sits in the DMZ for B2B/remote video firewall traversal, making it internet-adjacent.

stage-m19.text: Could CVE-2022-20812 be exploited fully unauthenticated?
options: No — it required cluster API credentials with read-write privileges
stage-m19.explanation: The attack needed read-write cluster API credentials — it was an authenticated flaw.

stage-m19.text: What coding control prevents path traversal like this?
options: Canonicalize and validate file paths against an allowlisted root
stage-m19.explanation: Normalizing paths and confining them to an allowed directory blocks ../ escapes.

stage-m19.text: Why does a DMZ-resident appliance with a traversal flaw warrant urgency?
options: It's reachable from less-trusted networks and can be a pivot into the core
stage-m19.explanation: DMZ devices straddle trust boundaries, so compromise can bridge external to internal networks.

stage-m19.text: Given the auth requirement, what reduces risk for cluster API credentials?
options: Strong, rotated credentials and least-privilege API accounts
stage-m19.explanation: Least-privilege, rotated credentials limit who can reach an authenticated write primitive.

stage-m20.text: CVE-2024-20399 exploited unsanitized parameters in which Cisco OS's CLI?
options: Cisco NX-OS — running on Nexus data center switches
stage-m20.explanation: The CLI argument injection affected NX-OS on Nexus switches.

stage-m20.text: Could unauthenticated attackers gain root via CVE-2024-20399?
options: No — authenticated access with privilege level 5+ was required
stage-m20.explanation: The vulnerable command required an authenticated account at privilege level 5 or higher.

stage-m20.text: What made CVE-2024-20399 dangerous despite needing authentication?
options: It broke out of NX-OS RBAC entirely, granting Linux root invisible to NX-OS monitoring
stage-m20.explanation: The injection escaped NX-OS RBAC to the underlying Linux OS as root, evading NX-OS visibility.

stage-m20.text: How long did Velvet Ant persist in the data center switches before detection?
stage-m20.explanation: The Velvet Ant APT hid in the core switches for roughly three years undetected.

stage-m20.text: Why does persistence in /etc/rc.d/rc.local survive NX-OS upgrades?
options: The underlying Linux init scripts persist independently of the NX-OS software layer
stage-m20.explanation: The Linux OS beneath NX-OS retains its init scripts across NX-OS software updates.

stage-m20.text: Why is escaping NX-OS RBAC to Linux root so impactful for defenders?
options: Activity at the Linux layer is invisible to NX-OS logging and controls
stage-m20.explanation: Operating below the NX-OS abstraction evades the device's own monitoring and policy.

stage-m20.text: What helps detect a Linux-layer implant on a Nexus switch?
options: File integrity baselines and external telemetry rather than NX-OS 'show' commands alone
stage-m20.explanation: Because NX-OS can't see the implant, integrity checks and out-of-band telemetry are needed.

stage-m20.text: What reduces the risk of an authenticated CLI-injection flaw like this?
options: Tight privilege management, limiting level-5+ accounts, and monitoring privileged use
stage-m20.explanation: Minimizing high-privilege accounts and watching their use shrinks the exploit population.

stage-m21.text: In ArcaneDoor, CVE-2024-20353 crashed Cisco ASA firewalls. What was the primary operational goal?
options: Clear in-memory logs to create a detection blind spot before follow-on operations
stage-m21.explanation: The crash wiped volatile logs, blinding defenders ahead of further intrusion steps.

stage-m21.text: Did CVE-2024-20353 require valid admin credentials?
options: No — the malformed multipart HTTP request was sendable by an unauthenticated attacker
stage-m21.explanation: The crashing request needed no authentication, making it remotely triggerable.

stage-m21.text: What CVE combination did ArcaneDoor use for persistent access?
options: CVE-2024-20353 (DoS/log wipe) chained with CVE-2024-20359 (persistent backdoor)
stage-m21.explanation: The crash/log-wipe was chained with the persistence flaw to install a durable backdoor quietly.

stage-m21.text: Which threat actor was attributed to ArcaneDoor?
options: UAT4356 — a Chinese state-sponsored APT (also tracked as Storm-1849)
stage-m21.explanation: The espionage campaign was attributed to UAT4356 / Storm-1849, a Chinese state actor.

stage-m21.text: Does forwarding ASA syslogs to an external SIEM prevent log loss from this crash?
options: Yes — externally forwarded syslogs survive the reload; only the in-memory buffer is lost
No, SIEM logs are also wiped
stage-m21.explanation: External syslog forwarding preserves evidence the device's volatile buffer loses on crash.

stage-m21.text: Why would an attacker crash a firewall specifically to clear in-memory logs?
options: To remove evidence of intrusion and create a blind spot for follow-on activity
stage-m21.explanation: Destroying volatile logs hampers detection and forensics during the operation.

stage-m21.text: What is the key defensive takeaway from the log-wipe technique?
options: Centralize logs off-device so evidence survives appliance crashes
Keep logs only on the device
stage-m21.explanation: Off-device log centralization defeats on-device log destruction.

stage-m21.text: Why are perimeter firewalls prime targets for nation-state espionage like ArcaneDoor?
options: They see all ingress/egress traffic and sit outside many internal monitoring tools
stage-m21.explanation: Edge firewalls offer a powerful vantage point and often escape internal EDR coverage.

stage-m22.text: The Line Dancer implant (CVE-2024-20359) survived ASA reboots by abusing what?
options: A VPN client/plug-in pre-loading mechanism that executed files from flash on boot
stage-m22.explanation: It abused a legacy plug-in preload feature to run attacker code from flash at boot.

stage-m22.text: Did Line Dancer appear in the ASA 'show run' configuration output?
options: No — it operated entirely in memory and was invisible to standard 'show' commands
stage-m22.explanation: The implant ran in memory, leaving no trace in the visible running configuration.

stage-m22.text: How did Line Dancer authenticate its operator to avoid accidental discovery?
options: A 'host knock' — only HTTPS POSTs with a specific Host header value triggered shellcode
stage-m22.explanation: A magic Host header value acted as a covert knock to activate the implant.

stage-m22.text: What monitoring did Line Dancer disable on the compromised ASA?
stage-m22.explanation: It turned off syslog forwarding and SNMP traps to suppress alerting during operations.

stage-m22.text: Are standard 'show' commands sufficient to detect Line Dancer?
options: No — detection required comparing flash file hashes against Cisco-signed baselines
stage-m22.explanation: Only forensic flash hash comparison against signed baselines reliably exposed the implant.

stage-m22.text: Why does an in-memory implant frustrate standard incident response?
options: It leaves little persistent on-disk evidence and evades config/log review
It is easy to dump with 'show run'
stage-m22.explanation: Memory-resident malware avoids the artifacts IR teams normally inspect.

stage-m22.text: Why are Cisco-signed file hash baselines critical against implants like this?
options: They let defenders detect unauthorized modifications the device itself won't reveal
stage-m22.explanation: Comparing against signed baselines surfaces tampering invisible to normal device commands.

stage-m22.text: What is the defensive implication of an implant disabling syslog/SNMP locally?
options: Telemetry must be collected and validated off-device, not trusted on-box
stage-m22.explanation: Since the implant silences on-box telemetry, off-device collection and integrity checks are essential.

stage-m23.text: CVE-2021-1291 is a SQL injection in which Cisco SD-WAN component?
options: Cisco vManage — the centralized SD-WAN management/orchestration controller
stage-m23.explanation: The SQLi was in vManage, the SD-WAN control and orchestration plane.

stage-m23.text: What privilege did an attacker need to extract router PSKs via CVE-2021-1291?
options: A low-privilege read-only account was sufficient
stage-m23.explanation: A read-only account could exploit the injection — a low bar for a catastrophic outcome.

stage-m23.text: What SQL injection technique exfiltrated data from additional tables?
options: UNION-based injection — appending a second SELECT to retrieve out-of-scope data
stage-m23.explanation: A UNION-based payload appended a SELECT to pull data from tables beyond the original query.

stage-m23.text: Why is compromising vManage catastrophic for an SD-WAN environment?
options: It's the single pane of glass — control it and you control every WAN router, policy, and tunnel
stage-m23.explanation: vManage orchestrates the entire fabric; owning it means owning the whole WAN.

stage-m23.text: After a confirmed vManage SQLi compromise, is patching vManage alone sufficient?
options: No — all WAN router PSKs and tunnel credentials should be rotated, since all were potentially exposed
stage-m23.explanation: Because the controller holds all router secrets, those credentials must be rotated post-incident.

stage-m23.text: What architectural property makes vManage such a high-value target?
options: Centralized control concentrates trust — one compromise cascades enterprise-wide
stage-m23.explanation: Centralizing control also centralizes risk: a single breach has fabric-wide blast radius.

stage-m23.text: What coding practice prevents SQL injection like CVE-2021-1291?
options: Parameterized queries / prepared statements with input validation
stage-m23.explanation: Parameterized queries separate code from data, defeating UNION and other injections.

stage-m23.text: Why doesn't a 'read-only' account make a management platform safe?
options: An injection flaw can let even a read-only user reach data and actions beyond their role
stage-m23.explanation: A vulnerability can elevate a low-privilege account's reach, so least privilege isn't a substitute for patching.

stage-m24.text: CVE-2023-20109 exploited the GDOI protocol used by which Cisco VPN feature?
options: GET VPN (Group Encrypted Transport VPN) — using group-shared keys across members
stage-m24.explanation: GET VPN uses GDOI group keying; the flaw was in that group key distribution.

stage-m24.text: Could compromising the GET VPN key server affect all group members at once?
options: Yes — the key server broadcasts GDOI rekeys, so one malicious rekey exploits all members
stage-m24.explanation: A single malicious rekey reaches every group member simultaneously via the key server.

stage-m24.text: Which GDOI rekey attribute contained the field that triggered the overflow?
options: The KEK (Key Encryption Key) attribute, not properly validated by member routers
stage-m24.explanation: Members failed to validate the KEK attribute, enabling the buffer overflow on rekey.

stage-m24.text: What delivery mechanism reached all group members simultaneously?
options: IP multicast — GDOI rekeys sent to a multicast group address like 239.0.0.1
stage-m24.explanation: Rekeys use IP multicast, so one message hits the entire group at once.

stage-m24.text: What practice limits the blast radius of a key server compromise?
options: Segmenting GET VPN groups by criticality so fewer routers share a key domain
Putting all routers in one group
stage-m24.explanation: Smaller, criticality-based groups mean a malicious rekey affects fewer routers.

stage-m24.text: Why does a shared group-key model create concentrated risk?
options: Compromising the key distribution point can poison every member sharing that key
stage-m24.explanation: Shared keying centralizes trust in the key server, so its compromise cascades to all members.

stage-m24.text: What member-side control would have blunted CVE-2023-20109?
options: Proper validation of received rekey attributes before processing
stage-m24.explanation: Validating the KEK and other rekey fields before use prevents the overflow.

stage-m24.text: Why is hardening and monitoring the GET VPN key server a priority?
options: It is the single point that, if compromised, can attack the whole VPN group
stage-m24.explanation: As the source of group rekeys, the key server is the chokepoint whose compromise is enterprise-wide.

stage-m25.text: CVE-2018-0296 was a path traversal in which component of the Cisco ASA?
options: The ASA web interface — via /+CSCOE+/ and /+CSCOT+/ URL paths
stage-m25.explanation: The traversal abused the ASA WebVPN URL paths /+CSCOE+/ and /+CSCOT+/.

stage-m25.text: Did CVE-2018-0296 only cause a denial of service?
options: No — it also enabled directory listing of the ASA filesystem (modules, VPN plugins)
stage-m25.explanation: Besides crashing the device, it disclosed filesystem directory contents.

stage-m25.text: What encoding technique let CVE-2018-0296 bypass naive input filters?
options: Percent-encoding — %2F for '/' and %2E%2E for '..' to evade literal-character checks
stage-m25.explanation: URL percent-encoding of slashes and dots evaded filters looking for literal traversal characters.

stage-m25.text: How quickly did automated scanners begin probing for CVE-2018-0296?
options: Within 48 hours — mass scanning began almost immediately after the June 2018 advisory
stage-m25.explanation: Mass scanning kicked off within roughly two days of the advisory's publication.

stage-m25.text: What happens during the ~3-minute ASA reload caused by CVE-2018-0296?
options: All stateful inspection, ACLs, and VPN tunnels are suspended
stage-m25.explanation: During reload the firewall enforces no ACLs or VPN inspection — a security gap.

stage-m25.text: Why did naive character-based input filtering fail here?
options: Attackers encoded the traversal characters, so literal-match filters missed them
stage-m25.explanation: Robust handling must decode/canonicalize input before validation, not match raw literals.

stage-m25.text: Why is a firewall reload a security concern, not just availability?
options: While down, the firewall isn't enforcing policy, briefly exposing the network
stage-m25.explanation: A reloading firewall leaves a window with no inspection or ACL enforcement.

stage-m25.text: What is the remediation for CVE-2018-0296?
options: Patch the ASA software and restrict web interface exposure
stage-m25.explanation: Patching the traversal flaw and limiting web-interface exposure closes the vulnerability.
